claim: The high hapax ratio (70.1%) is substantially produced by a systematic suffix morphology where word-final glyphs {y, n, l, r, o} function as inflectional suffixes on a smaller stem vocabulary, such that stripping final glyphs from the set {y, n, l, r, o} would reduce the unique word count by at least 35% while preserving a stem vocabulary with Zipf exponent closer to 1.0.
evidence_for: Word-final glyph constraints show {y,n,l,r,o} dominate endings, consistent with suffix behavior
Hapax ratio of 70.1% is anomalously high and could reflect surface-form inflation by productive suffixation
Zipf exponent 0.8946 is below natural language norms (~1.0), consistent with a vocabulary inflated by morphological variants
evidence_against: If suffixes encode cipher elements rather than morphology, stripping them may destroy meaningful units
High confidence beliefs already suggest positional constraints reflect cipher structure rather than grammar (0.813)
Suffix stripping across Currier A and B may yield different stem sets, complicating unified analysis
test: Strip all word-final glyphs belonging to {y, n, l, r, o} from every token in the corpus; recompute vocabulary size, hapax ratio, and Zipf exponent on the resulting stem forms; compare Zipf exponent before and after stripping for each Currier sub-corpus separately.
test_metric: Post-stripping unique stem count relative to pre-stripping unique word count; post-stripping Zipf exponent; post-stripping hapax ratio
test_threshold: Confirm if post-stripping vocabulary reduction exceeds 35% and post-stripping Zipf exponent rises above 0.95; deny if reduction is below 15% or Zipf exponent does not improve

tests_run.test: strip all word-final glyphs belonging to {y, n, l, r, o} from every token in the corpus; recompute v
tests_run.details: Observed Zipf exponent: 0.8946 (R²=0.9084). Natural language expected: ~1.0. Fit quality: moderate.

reasoning: If suffix stripping substantially reduces hapax count and improves Zipf fit, it suggests the positional constraints encode morphological suffixes (or cipher suffix-equivalents) that inflate surface vocabulary, narrowing the cipher model toward a morphologically regular underlying language.
